system:
You receive two text fragments extracted from adjacent pages of a PDF document.
The page boundary may have split a sentence between them.

Determine if these fragments are parts of the same sentence or thought. If so,
merge them into one clean sentence. If they are clearly separate, say so.

Rules:
- If they form one sentence: output ONLY the merged text on a single line
- If they are separate sentences: output the word SEPARATE on its own line
- Remove any duplicated words or partial words at the merge point
- Do not add words that aren't in either fragment
- Preserve original wording exactly, except for merge-point cleanup
user:
Fragment A (end of page 1):
"The biomedical field remains an area with relatively few proposed benchmarks, mainly for English and Chinese, facilitating the availability of many biomedical models in these two languages."

Fragment B (start of page 2):
"In the case of specialized domains, general benchmarks may not adequately evaluate the performance of in-domain models."

user:
Fragment A (end of page 3):
"Due to the presence of nested entities in annotations, we simplified the evaluation process by retaining only annotations at the higher granularity level from the BigBio (Fries et al., 2022) implementation, following the approach described in Touchent et al. (2023), which translates into an average loss of 6.06% of"

Fragment B (start of page 4):
"the annotations on EMEA and 8.90% on MEDLINE. Additionally, considering that some documents from EMEA exceed the maximum input sequence length that most current language models can handle, we decided to split these documents into sentences."
output: Due to the presence of nested entities in annotations, we simplified the evaluation process by retaining only annotations at the higher granularity level from the BigBio (Fries et al., 2022) implementation, following the approach described in Touchent et al. (2023), which translates into an average loss of 6.06% of the annotations on EMEA and 8.90% on MEDLINE. Additionally, considering that some documents from EMEA exceed the maximum input sequence length that most current language models can handle, we decided to split these documents into sentences.

user:
Fragment A (end of page 5):
"All the models are fine-tuned regarding a strict protocol using the same hyperparameters for each downstream task. The reported results are obtained by averaging the scores from four separate runs, thus ensuring robustness and reliability. We also report statistical significance computed using Student's t-test."

Fragment B (start of page 6):
"To ensure a fair and consistent comparison among systems for sequence-to-sequence tasks such as POS tagging and NER, we chose the SeqEval (Nakayama, 2018) metric in conjunction with the IOB2 format and the training of all the models to predict only the label on the first token of each word as mentioned by Touchent et al. (2023)."
